author: @thewatersavior
text: Seems like a weird place where renewables could help "lower" the activation energy by using excess grid energy specifically for these processes. I am guessing the conversion losses may be high but I also imagine that there is a ton of land available right on dump sites that could be used for solar.

replies.author: @drillerdev4624
replies.text:
There's already a bacteria that has been found that can biodegrade polyestyrene (probably misspelled). It is found in the digestive system of the larvae form of a certain japanese beetle. They can be fed chunks of the white balls and they tore them down, secret small parts, and then go at it again. And as long as you keep them fed and together, they won't metamorphose into their adult state. The bacteria in their gut help process the plastic to the point that apparently, they have no traces of microplastics in them, and can be used to feed animals.

The Thought Emporium had a cool video about it, check it out.

author: @D-B-Cooper
text: Amazing how that sediment of organic life ends up 6000 feet below the surface.

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
Time Team dug up a camp in an English village where US troops had waited for the D-Day; in only eight decades the area had been covered with over a foot of soil despite weather and human activity on the site hindering buildup. The layers fossil fuels are found in formed 300+ million years ago, there is no mystery how they are so deep.
But over such long time plenty of sites that contained organic material or already formed fossil fuels would have been unearthed by erosion or earthquakes. Gas would have simply escaped, oil seeps on the surface and breaks down or forms other compounds. Coal weathers away and returns to natural cycle. If a storm passes by and lightning strikes close enough they will burn away. Over time continental drift pushes the deposits deeper under another plate. Even without humans burning them fossil fuels would have disappeared eventually.

replies.author: @D-B-Cooper
replies.text: @AnalystPrime time team is an embarrassment to archeology. In the same area you can also find Roman artifacts, Bronze Age artifacts and also Stone Age objects a foot underground. If oil is from algae drifting to the sea bed then had 6000 ft of inorganic sediment accumulate on top of it you need to question if the ocean was then sterile for millions of years. Geologists don’t claim this theory and have different explanation for why an oil well will refill in time. In New Zealand in a desolate part of it they dig up the most expensive wood on the planet they find huge logs from the primordial forest, partly silica, partly wood. Not yet petrified but definitely not turning into oil.

replies.author: @DM-kl4em
replies.text:
I hear you, but the problem is that it happened millions of years ago.  An asteroid hit the Earth and wiped out the dinosaurs (and almost all plant and animal life).  The huge amount of dead plants and animals turned into the massive coal, oil, and gas reserves that we have today.

We currently use those reserves at an alarming rate.  Another asteroid would certainly replace it, but it would create a whole different set of problems.  However, if some of us survived, and rebuilt society, I wonder how long it would take for the resulting fossil fuels to be ready for use.  It might not take millions of years, and it certainly would not lie undiscovered for millions of years.

replies.author: @AnalystPrime
replies.text:
It takes millions of years to form a solid impermeable layer over the organic material that keeps it airtight and presses down with enough weight to cause heat(or alternatively push the material down to depths where the temperature is high enough) for the process to be possible in the first place.
And yes, there are machines that can turn waste into oil by using huge amount of heat to produce something like a gallon per day, which means you need to run that machine for over a century just to produce as much oil as a relatively small well will pump up in one day, or for ten million years to produce as much oil as the world uses in a day.

author: @DeliciousDeBlair
text:
I designed a super simple solar powered pyrolysis system, and have some of the parts I need to build it.

Its not super simple, no, but its super efficient, because it works based on simply focusing concentrated solar power on the working space with a combination of a borosilicate glass outer shell [for safety] and a fused quartz tube or a single crystal corundum tube on the inside [where the heat is].

Quartz is the easier one to get, and a bit cheaper, while simple fused alumina or zirconia can handle more heat, but typically are too opaque to really deliver the solar energy directly into the working material, and therefore too prone to re-radiate the heat as IR radiation.

Single crystal alumina or zirconia, while possible, tend to be quite pricey in large pieces [keeping in mind that one of the many fake versions of diamonds is cubic zirconia], and while single crystal silicon carbide is known to exist, I do not know how large it is even possible to make it, so I do not know if it, or silicon nitride can even be considered.

On the other hand, also, I do not know if there are any IR emissivity or transmissivity tests conducted on them for such purposes yet.

So far as a [relatively] cheap, and yet wholly reusable catalyst, cerium oxide, especially mixed with some calcium oxide, simply mingled into the ground up feedstock can really provide some excellent catalytic reaction.

Hydrogen can be added as urea, with the additional benefit of helping keep the product fluid, and driving off stray oxygen, and the nitrogen can be used to create cyannogens out of otherwise solid waste carbons, which, while quite toxic, are also useful precursors to many chemical feedstocks, and can even be used as fuels, or converted into explosives for the mining industry.

It is not IF it can be done, its been done for over a century already.

Its how profitable it can be done, and on what scale.

I would love if there were laws mandating that all waste plastics be recycled this way, but to implement that, to say the least, would be pretty near impossible, unless every step of the process were paid for somehow, and that would either make taxation unbearable, or else require that the profits be shared along the whole chain of the cycle, or a company so huge that it could handle the whole process and remain profitable.

I had a design for [based on 1990's money] 2 billion USD that in fact, could, fully recycle every scrap of waste plastic in the whole of the United States, but there was no way to properly submit the paperwork to the right investors at that time, seeing as the drive back then did not exist, and the people who were supposed to be pushing it forward, were both old, and in bad health, and have since died.

Without those people, and with many other issues, the process remains a large business prospectus in PDF format, until, or unless I can prove it works just exactly how I said it would work.

I have a web domain dedicated to it, but its a fully empty site, and has been for a full decade.

If I ever begin producing any provable research, I will eventually begin posting it on that site.

I may try a crude version with only the borosilicate glass, but it may also melt and fail at those temperatures.

We shall see.
